notes.section_title: The Breakup of the Maurya Empire

content.text: After Emperor Aśhoka, the Mauryan successors were unable to maintain control over the vast empire. In 185 BCE, the last Mauryan emperor, Brihadratha, was assassinated by his commander-in-chief, Puṣhyamitra Śhunga.
The period following the Mauryas is called the 'Age of Reorganization' because existing regions were reshaped into new, competing kingdoms that eventually became quite powerful.
Create a timeline starting from the 2nd century BCE to the 3rd century CE. Mark the major dynasties and events as you study this chapter.

Puṣhyamitra Śhunga founded the Śhunga dynasty in North and Central India. Although his empire was smaller than the Mauryan one, he maintained security and established friendly relations with the Greeks after initial conflicts.

content.title: Key Features of the Śhunga Period
items: Revival of Vedic rituals, including the Aśhvamedha yajña (horse sacrifice).
Patronage of art and architecture, notably the Bharhut Stūpa in Madhya Pradesh.
Emergence of Sanskrit as a preferred language for literature and philosophy.
Compilation of the Yoga Sūtras by the scholar Patañjali.

content.text: The Bharhut Stūpa features beautiful carvings depicting stories from the life of the Buddha, representing some of the earliest examples of Buddhist art.

The Sātavāhanas ruled large parts of the Deccan (present-day Maharashtra, Telangana, and Andhra Pradesh) from the 2nd century BCE onward. Their famous capitals included Amarāvatī and Pratiṣhṭhāna (Paithan).

content.title: Economic Life under the Sātavāhanas
rows: Flourished internally and with the Roman Empire (spices, textiles, luxury items).
Depicted ships with masts on coins, signifying advanced shipbuilding and navigation.
Prospered near the Krishna-Godavari river system, providing stability.
Collected through tolls and taxes at resting places like the Naneghat caves.

content.text: Sātavāhana traditions often included naming princes after their mothers, such as Gautamīputra Sātakarṇi (son of Queen Gautamī Balaśhrī), highlighting the influence of powerful women.
After the Mauryan decline, Kalinga rose to prominence under the Chedi dynasty. Their most famous king, Khāravela, was a devoted follower of Jainism but respected all schools of thought.

content.title: Legacy of the Chedis
items: The Hāthīgumphā inscription records Khāravela's military victories and benevolent works.
Construction of the Udayagiri-Khandagiri caves for Jain monks near Bhubaneswar.
Mastery of 'rock-cut architecture', where rooms and panels were carved with high precision directly into rocky hillsides.

notes.section_title: Kingdoms and Life in the South

topics.topic_name: The Three Crowned Kings and Sangam Era

content.text: The southern region saw the rise of the Cheras, Cholas, and Pānḍyas. This era is known as the 'Sangam Age' after the 'Sangams' (assemblies) of poets who composed a vast body of literature.

content.title: Major Southern Kingdoms
rows: King Karikāla built the Grand Anicut (Kallaṇai) dam; rich in trade.
Hub for spices, timber, and ivory; extensive Roman trade.
Prosperous naval power known for high-quality pearls.

content.text: The epic 'Silappadikāram' centers on the principles of justice and the ruler's duty (dharma), telling the story of Kaṇṇagi and Kovalan.
Invaders from the northwest, like the Indo-Greeks and Śhakas, settled in India and were deeply influenced by local culture, leading to a unique blend in governance and art.

content.title: Cultural Interactions
items: The Heliodorus pillar near Vidisha shows a Greek ambassador's devotion to the Indian deity Vāsudeva.
Indo-Greek coins featured both Greek and Indian deities like Vāsudeva-Kṛiṣhṇa and Lakṣhmī.
The Śhakas introduced the Śhaka Samvat calendar (starts 78 CE), which is now India's National Calendar.

topics.topic_name: King Kaṇiṣhka and the Silk Route

content.text: Originating from Central Asia, the Kuṣhāṇas established a vast empire in North India. King Kaṇiṣhka, their most famous ruler, controlled significant portions of the Silk Route, facilitating trade between India and the West.
Kaṇiṣhka's coins reflect shared values, featuring both the emperor and religious figures like the Buddha or Śhiva with the bull Nandi.
Observe the headless statue of Kaṇiṣhka. Notice the clothing, heavy footwear, and weapons. What do these tell you about the origins and power of the Kuṣhāṇa rulers?
The Kuṣhāṇa period encouraged the development of two major schools of art, each with distinct characteristics.

content.title: Comparison of Art Schools
rows: Fusion of Indian, Greek, and Roman elements.

content.text: Despite political struggles, this age was one of cultural assimilation, seen in the flourishing of Sanskrit literature and the final composition of epics like the Mahābhārata and Rāmāyana.

glossary.definition: A peaceful political alliance arranged through a marriage between members of different royal families.
A Vedic horse sacrifice ritual conducted by rulers to declare their supreme position as an emperor.
Derived from 'sangha', it refers to an association or assembly of poets in South India.
Provincial governors or local rulers left behind by conquerors to manage territories.
An architectural style where structures, chambers, and carvings are sculpted directly into natural solid rock.
A historic network of trade routes connecting China with the Mediterranean world through Central and South Asia.
A type of crystalline rock, specifically the grey-black variety used in Gandhara sculptures.

summary_points: The decline of the Mauryas in 185 BCE led to a period of 'Reorganization', where multiple native and foreign dynasties emerged.
Native dynasties like the Śhungas, Sātavāhanas, and Chedis revived Vedic rituals and patronized Buddhist and Jain schools.
The Southern kingdoms (Cholas, Cheras, Pānḍyas) flourished through maritime trade and produced the rich Sangam literature.
Invasions by Indo-Greeks and Śhakas resulted in deep cultural assimilation, blending Greek aesthetics with local Indian traditions.
The Kuṣhāṇas, under King Kaṇiṣhka, controlled international trade via the Silk Route and fostered the Gāndhāra and Mathurā art schools.
Technological advancements like advanced shipbuilding and rock-cut precision engineering characterized this era's progress.
By the 3rd century CE, empires began to fragment, paving the way for further regional powers in the Indian subcontinent.
